name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلصِّيَامُ كَمَا كُتِبَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ (١٨٣)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those who were before you, so that you may be Godwary. (183)
```

```arabic
أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۚ فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُطِيقُونَهُۥ فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ ۖ فَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَن تَصُومُوا۟ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٤)
```

```english
That for known days. But should any of you be sick or on a journey, let it be a [similar] number of other days. Those who find it straining shall be liable to atonement by feeding a needy person. Should anyone do good of his own accord, that is better for him, and to fast is better for you, should you know. (184)
```

```arabic
شَهْرُ رَمَضَانَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ فِيهِ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ وَبَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَٱلْفُرْقَانِ ۚ فَمَن شَهِدَ مِنكُمُ ٱلشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ مَرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ ۗ يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ بِكُمُ ٱلْيُسْرَ وَلَا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ ٱلْعُسْرَ وَلِتُكْمِلُوا۟ ٱلْعِدَّةَ وَلِتُكَبِّرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَىٰكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٨٥)
```

```english
The month of Ramadan is one in which the Quran was sent down as guidance to mankind, with manifest proofs of guidance and the Criterion. So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it, and as for someone who is sick or on a journey, let it be a [similar] number of other days. Allah desires ease for you, and He does not desire hardship for you, and so that you may complete the number and magnify Allah for guiding you, and that you may give thanks. (185)
```

## (Commentary)

The context of these three verses indicates, firstly, that they were all revealed together. His saying, the Exalted: "for a fixed number of days" (أياماً معدودات) at the beginning of the second verse is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) related to His saying: "fasting" (الصيام) in the first verse. His saying, the Exalted: "the month of Ramaḍān" (شهر رمضان) in the third verse is either the predicate (خبر) of an omitted subject (مبتدأ)—which is the pronoun referring back to His saying "for a fixed number of days," the implied meaning being: "they are the month of Ramaḍān"—or it is a subject for an omitted predicate, the implied meaning being: "the month of Ramaḍān is that whose fasting has been prescribed for you." Alternatively, it is an appositive (بدل) for "fasting" (الصيام) in His saying: "Prescribed for you is fasting" in the first verse. Regardless of the grammatical interpretation, it serves as an exposition and clarification (بيان وإيضاح) of the "fixed number of days" in which fasting was prescribed. Thus, the three verses together constitute a single discourse aimed at a single purpose: explaining the obligation of fasting the month of Ramaḍān.

Secondly, the context of the verses indicates that a portion of the speech placed in these three verses serves as a preamble and introduction (توطئة وتمهيد) for another portion. By this, I mean that the first two verses were composed to serve as a preface intended to calm the souls and secure their tranquility and stability against anxiety and agitation. This is done when the speaker's aim is to convey something for which non-compliance or refusal to accept is feared, because the forthcoming ruling or news is inherently heavy and difficult for the addressee. Therefore, you see that the first two verses are composed of sentences, none of which are devoid of guiding the mind of the addressee toward the legislation of the Ramaḍān fast with gentleness and suitability. This is achieved by mentioning that which removes estrangement and agitation, brings about the soul's contentment, and breaks the surge of defiance and arrogance, by pointing to types of mitigation and facilitation (تخفيف وتسهيل) observed in the legislation of this ruling, alongside the immediate and deferred good it contains.

Therefore, when He mentioned the prescription of fasting upon them by saying: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيَامُ** "O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fasting," He followed it with His saying: **كَمَا كُتِبَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ** "as it was prescribed for those who were before you." That is: you should not find it burdensome or feel estranged by its legislation for you and its prescription upon you, for this ruling is not restricted to you; rather, it is a ruling established for the nations prior to you, and you are not unique in this. Furthermore, in acting upon this ruling, there is the hope for what you seek and desire through your faith, which is godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah and guarding oneself against His displeasure* — which is the best provision for one who believes in Allah and the Last Day, and you are the believers. This is His saying, the Exalted: **لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ** "so that you may be God-conscious." Moreover, this act, in which there is hope for godwariness for you and those before you, does not consume all your time, nor most of it; rather, it is only for a few, specific, numbered days. This is His saying, the Exalted: **أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَاتٍ** "for a fixed number of days." Indeed, the use of the indefinite form (تنكير) for "days" (أياماً) indicates minimization (تحقير), and describing them as "numbered" (معدودات) highlights the ease of the matter, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍ بَخْسٍ دَرَاهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ** "And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams" (Yusuf: 20). Furthermore, We have considered the side of those for whom the essence of fasting is difficult, such as those who can only fast with extreme hardship; such a person must replace it with a ransom (فدية) that is neither difficult nor burdensome for him, which is the feeding of a needy person. This is His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَن كَانَ مِنكُم مَّرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ** "So should any of you be sick or on a journey..." until His saying: **فِدْيَةٌ طَعَامُ مِسْكِينٍ** "...a redemption by feeding a needy person." If this act contains your own good and observes as much mitigation and facilitation for you as possible, then it is better for you to perform it with obedience and desire, without dislike, heaviness, or hesitation. For whoever does good of his own accord, it is better for him than performing it out of compulsion. This is His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَن تَطَوَّعَ خَيْرًا فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُ** "And whoever does good of his own accord, it is better for him." Thus, the discourse placed in the two verses, as you see, is a preamble and introduction for His saying, the Exalted, in the third verse: **فَمَن شَهِدَ مِنكُمُ الشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ** "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it."

Accordingly, His saying, the Exalted, in the first verse: "Prescribed for you is fasting" (كتب عليكم الصيام) is an informative statement (إخبار) about the realization of the prescription, not the initial enactment (إنشاء) of the ruling, as in His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْقِصَاصُ فِي الْقَتْلَى** "O you who have faith! Retribution is prescribed for you regarding the slain" (al-Baqarah: 178), and His saying, the Exalted: **كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا الْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوَالِدَيْنِ وَالْأَقْرَبِينَ** "Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and if he leaves any property, is that he make a bequest for his parents and near relatives" (al-Baqarah: 180). For there is a difference between retribution for the slain and bequests for parents and relatives, and fasting. Retribution for the slain is something that accords with the sense of revenge raging in the souls of the victims' guardians, and it suits the instinctive stinginess in human nature to see the killer alive, safe, and living without regard for the murder he committed. Similarly, the sense of compassion and mercy for kin calls the souls to show mercy to parents and relatives, especially at the time of death and permanent separation. Thus, these two—retribution and bequests—are rulings naturally accepted by human dispositions, agreeing with what they necessitate. Therefore, informing about them by enacting them does not require the preparation of a preface or the preamble of an exposition. This is in contrast to the ruling of fasting, for it necessitates depriving the souls of their greatest desires and most of what they incline toward, namely eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse. Therefore, it is heavy upon the nature and disliked by the soul. Directing its ruling to the addressees—who are the general mass of the legally responsible (المكلفين)—requires a preparation and preamble to gladden their souls and cause their natures to yearn for its acceptance and adoption. For this reason, His saying: "Retribution is prescribed for you..." and His saying: "Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you..." were enactments (إنشاء) of the ruling without the need for a preparatory preface, unlike His saying: "Prescribed for you is fasting," which is an informative report about the ruling and a preparation for its enactment in His saying: "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it," through the sum of the seven segments contained in the two verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أيها الذين آمنوا** (O you who have faith!): Bringing this address is to remind them of an attribute within them, which is faith (الإيمان). When they attend to this attribute, they must accept the ruling their Lord presents to them, even if it is contrary to their desires and habits. The verse of retribution also began with this for the reason you have heard—that the Christians used to favor pardon over retribution, even though other religious and non-religious groups upheld retribution.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كتب عليكم الصيام كما كتب على الذين من قبلكم** (Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those who were before you): "Writing" (الكتابة) is well-known in meaning, and it is used as a metonym for obligation (الفرض), determination (العزيمة), and the final decree (القضاء الحتم), as in His saying: **كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي** "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’" (al-Mujadilah: 21), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَدَّمُوا وَآثَارَهُمْ** "and We write what they have sent ahead and their effects" (Ya Sin: 12), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيهَا أَنَّ النَّفْسَ بِالنَّفْسِ** "And in it We prescribed for them: a life for a life" (al-Ma'idah: 45).

"Fasting" (الصيام) and (الصوم) are linguistically two verbal nouns meaning to abstain (الكف) from an action, such as abstaining from eating, drinking, sexual intercourse, speaking, walking, and so on. It is sometimes said that it is specifically abstaining from what the soul desires and yearns for. Then its usage became predominant in the Sacred Law (الشرع) for abstaining from specific things from the break of dawn until sunset with intention (النية).

The meaning of "those who were before you" (الذين من قبلكم) refers to the past nations among those who preceded the appearance of Islam from the nations of the prophets, such as the nations of Moses, Jesus, and others. This meaning is what is customary from the use of this phrase in the Quran wherever it appears. His saying: "as it was prescribed for those who were before you" is not in a position of absolute generality regarding individuals, nor is it for exact comparison (التنظير). Thus, it does not indicate that fasting was prescribed for all the nations of the prophets without exception, nor that the fasting prescribed for them was the same as the fasting prescribed for us in terms of time, specifics, and attributes. The comparison in the verse is only in terms of the essence of fasting and abstention, not in terms of its specifics.

What is meant by "those who were before you" are the previous nations of religious people (المليين) in general. The Quran did not specify who they were, except that the apparent meaning of His saying: "as it was prescribed" is that these were people of a religion (الملة) upon whom this was obligated. There is nothing in the Torah and Gospel currently in the hands of the Jews and Christians that indicates the obligation and prescription of fasting; rather, the two books only praise it and magnify its importance. However, they fast for a fixed number of days in the year until today in various forms: such as fasting from meat, fasting from milk, and fasting from eating and drinking. In the Quran, there is the story of Zechariah's fast from speaking, and likewise Mary's fast from speaking.

Rather, fasting is a worship (عبادة) — *the act of humble submission and devotion to Allah* — inherited from even non-religious peoples, as is reported from ancient Egypt, ancient Greece, and the Romans. The idolaters of India fast even today. Indeed, its being an act of worship that brings one closer (قربية) is among the things man is guided to by his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — as will follow.

It is sometimes said that "those who were before you" refers to the Jews and Christians, or the previous prophets, based on narrations that are not free from weakness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لعلكم تتقون** (so that you may be God-conscious): The idolaters used to fast to please their gods or to extinguish the fire of their anger if they committed a crime or a sin, or when they wanted a need to be fulfilled. This makes fasting a transaction and an exchange whereby the servant gives the Lord's need so that He may fulfill the servant's need, or obtains His pleasure so that He may obtain the servant's pleasure. But Allah, the Glorified, is far too exalted for poverty, need, being affected, or harm to be conceived in His right. In short, He, the Glorified, is free from every deficiency. Thus, whatever beautiful effect worship gives—any worship and any effect—it only returns to the servant, not to the Lord, the Exalted and Sanctified. Just as sins are likewise; the Exalted said: **إِنْ أَحْسَنتُمْ أَحْسَنتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَهَا** "If you do good, you will do good to your own souls, and if you do evil, it will be [to their detriment]" (al-Isra: 7). This is what the Noble Quran points to in its teaching by returning the effects of acts of obedience and disobedience to the human being, whose only state is poverty and need. The Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنتُمُ الْفُقَرَاءُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ** "O mankind! You are the ones who stand in need of Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient" (Fatir: 15).

He points to this specifically regarding fasting with His saying: "so that you may be God-conscious" (لعلكم تتقون). That godwariness (التقوى) is expected to be attained through fasting is something beyond doubt. Every human being feels by his primordial nature (الفطرة) that whoever wishes to connect with the world of purity and loftiness, and to ascend to the level of perfection and spirituality, must first refrain from indulging in physical pleasures, restrain himself from the defiance of bodily desires, and sanctify himself from clinging to the earth. In short, he must guard against (يتقي) that which, by being occupied with it, distances him from the Lord, the Blessed and Exalted. This is a godwariness that is only attained through fasting and abstaining from desires. Closer than that, and more relevant to the state of the general mass of people—both those of this world and the hereafter—is to guard against the common trials of permissible desires like eating, drinking, and sexual intercourse, so that he may gain training in guarding against (اتقاء) prohibitions and avoiding them. Through this, his will is disciplined in abstaining from sins and drawing closer to Allah, the Glorified. For he who answers the caller of Allah regarding permissible desires and hears and obeys, will be even more hearing and obedient regarding the prohibitions and sins of Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أياماً معدودات** (for a fixed number of days): It is in the accusative case as an adverbial phrase (منصوب على الظرفية) with the implied preposition "in" (في), and it is related to His saying: "fasting" (الصيام). It has already been mentioned that the indefinite form of "days" and its description as "numbered" is to indicate the minimization of the task in terms of burden and hardship to encourage the legally responsible (المكلف). It has also been mentioned that His saying: "The month of Ramaḍān, in which the Quran was sent down..." is an exposition (بيان) of the "days," so the "fixed number of days" refers to the month of Ramaḍān.

Some exegetes have mentioned that the "fixed number of days" refers to three days of every month and the fast of the day of ʿĀshūrāʾ. Some said: the three days are the "white days" (الايام البيض) of every month and the fast of the day of ʿĀshūrāʾ; the Messenger of Allah and the Muslims used to fast them, then His saying, the Exalted: "The month of Ramaḍān, in which the Quran was sent down..." was revealed, so it abrogated that, and the obligation was settled on the fast of the month of Ramaḍān. They relied for this on many narrations from the paths of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jamāʿah* which are not themselves free from discrepancy and contradiction.

What makes the falsehood of this view apparent is, **firstly:** that fasting, as has been said, is a general and comprehensive worship. Had the matter been as they say, history would have recorded it, and no one would have differed regarding its establishment and then its abrogation. Furthermore, the attachment of the day of ʿĀshūrāʾ to the three days of every month in the obligation or recommendation of fasting—like its being one of the Islamic festivals—is among the innovations of the Umayyads (may Allah curse them). They annihilated the progeny of the Messenger of Allah and his household (أهل بيته) by killing their men, taking their women and children captive, and plundering their wealth in the Event of al-Ṭaff. Then they sought blessings from that day, took it as a festival, and legislated its fasting to seek blessings through it. They fabricated virtues and blessings for it and inserted hadiths indicating that it was an Islamic festival, rather, one of the general festivals known to the Arabs of the *Jāhiliyyah*, the Jews, and the Christians since the mission of Moses and Jesus. None of that was true. The day has no religious significance to become a religious-national festival like Nowruz or Mihrajān among the Persians, nor did any event of conquest or victory occur on it to make it an Islamic day like the Day of the Mission (المبعث) or the Prophet's birthday, nor does it have a religious aspect to become a religious festival like *ʿĪd al-Fiṭr* or *ʿĪd al-Aḍḥā*. So why should it be cherished without cause?

**Secondly:** The third verse of the verses—meaning His saying: "The month of Ramaḍān..."—refuses by its context to have been revealed alone as an abrogator (ناسخ) for what preceded it. The apparent context is that "the month of Ramaḍān" is the predicate of an omitted subject or a subject for an omitted predicate, as previously mentioned. Thus, it is an exposition (بيان) for the "fixed number of days," and all three verses are a single discourse aimed at a single purpose: the prescription of the Ramaḍān fast. As for making His saying "the month of Ramaḍān" a subject whose predicate is "in which the Quran was sent down," while this would necessitate the independence of the verse and its suitability to be revealed alone, it would not then be suitable to be an abrogator for what preceded it, due to the lack of contradiction between it and its predecessor—whereas abrogation (النسخ) is conditional upon contradiction and disparity.

Even weaker than this view is the view of others—as appears from them—that the second verse, meaning His saying, the Exalted: "for a fixed number of days," is an abrogator for the first verse, meaning His saying, the Exalted: "Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those who were before you." This is because fasting was prescribed for the Christians, then they added to it and subtracted from it after Jesus (peace be upon him) until it settled on fifty days. Then Allah legislated it for the Muslims through the first verse, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the people used to fast it in the early days of Islam until His saying, the Exalted: "for a fixed number of days" was revealed, so it abrogated the ruling and the ruling settled on something else.

This view is feebler than the previous one and its falsehood is more apparent. All the objections that apply to the previous view apply to it. That the second verse is a completion of the first verse is more apparent and clear. The narrations the proponent relied upon are more clearly in opposition to the apparent meaning of the Quran and the context of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فمن كان منكم مريضاً أو على سفر فعدة من أيام أُخر** (So should any of you be sick or on a journey, let it be a [fixed] number of other days): The "fa" (ف) is for derivation (التفريع), and the sentence is derived from His saying: "Prescribed for you" and His saying: "numbered." That is: fasting is prescribed and obligated, and the number is included in the obligation. Just as the hand is not removed from the essence of the obligation, it is not removed from the number. So if an occurrence arises that necessitates the removal of the obligatory ruling from the numbered days—which are the days of the month of Ramaḍān—such as the occurrence of sickness or travel, it does not remove the hand from fasting a number of other days outside the month of Ramaḍān equal in number to what the legally responsible person missed. This is what He, the Exalted, pointed to in the third verse with His saying: "and so that you may complete the number." Thus, His saying, the Exalted: "for a fixed number of days," just as it conveys the meaning of minimization as mentioned, conveys that the number is a pillar (ركن) included in the obligation and the ruling.

Furthermore, sickness (المرض) is the opposite of health, and travel (السفر) is taken from *al-safar* in the sense of uncovering (الكشف), as if the traveler is uncovered by his journey from his house where he takes shelter and is concealed. It is as if His saying, the Exalted: "or on a journey" (أو على سفر), rather than saying "a traveler" (مسافراً), is to point to the consideration of the actual engagement in the state at the moment, excluding the past and the future.

A group—the majority of the scholars of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jamāʿah*—have said: What is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: "So should any of you be sick or on a journey, let it be a [fixed] number of other days" is a concession (الرخصة) rather than a determination (العزيمة). Thus, the sick person and the traveler have a choice between fasting and breaking the fast. You have already known that the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: "let it be a [fixed] number of other days" is the determination of breaking the fast, not a concession. This is what is narrated from the Imams of the *Ahl al-Bayt*, and it is the school of a group of the Companions such as ʿAbd al-Raḥmān ibn ʿAwf, ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb, ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿUmar, Abū Hurayrah, and ʿUrwah ibn al-Zubayr. They are refuted by His saying, the Exalted: "let it be a [fixed] number of other days."

They have assumed an omission (تقدير) in the verse, saying: the implied meaning is "So should any of you be sick or on a journey *and breaks his fast*, then a number of other days."

This is refuted, **firstly:** because the omission, as they have stated, is contrary to the apparent meaning (خلاف الظاهر) and is not resorted to except with a contextual clue (قرينة), and there is no clue from the speech itself for it.

**Secondly:** The speech, even if the omission were granted, does not indicate a concession. For the context, as they mentioned, is a context of legislation (التشريع). Our saying: "So should any of you be sick or on a journey and breaks his fast," the most it indicates is that breaking the fast does not occur as a sin, but rather as permissible (جائزاً) in the broader sense of permissibility—encompassing obligation, recommendation, and mere permissibility. As for it being permissible in the sense of not being mandatory, there is absolutely no evidence for it from the speech; rather, the evidence is to the contrary. For the structure of speech in the context of legislation is based on not omitting what must be explained, which is unbecoming of the Wise Legislator, and this is apparent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وعلى الذين يطيقونه فدية طعام مسكين** (and for those who find it hard, a redemption by feeding a needy person): "Capability" (الاطاقة), as some have mentioned, is the expenditure of one's entire power in an action, and its necessity is that the action occurs with effort and hardship. The "ransom" (الفدية) is the substitute (البدل), and here it is a monetary substitute, which is the "feeding of a needy person" (طعام مسكين)—that is, food that satiates a hungry needy person from the average of what a person eats. The ruling of the ransom is also an obligation (فرض), like the ruling of making up the fast for the sick and the traveler, because of His saying: "and for those" (وعلى الذين), which is apparent in a specific obligation (الوجوب التعييني) rather than concession and choice.

Some have mentioned that the sentence conveys a concession and was then abrogated. Thus, He, the Glorified and Exalted, gave all those capable of fasting—meaning those able to fast—the choice between fasting and breaking the fast and expiating for every day by feeding a needy person, because the people at that time were not accustomed to fasting. Then that was abrogated by His saying: "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it." Some of these have mentioned: it abrogated the ruling for those who are not incapacitated; as for the likes of the elderly man, the pregnant woman, and the nursing mother, they remained in their state of the permissibility of the ransom.

By my life, this is nothing but playing with the Quran and making its verses into fragments (عضين). If you reflect on the three verses, you will find them to be a discourse established for a single purpose, with a single context, consistent sentences, and elegant exposition. Then, if you apply this discourse—in its unity and consistency—to what this proponent sees, you will find its context disrupted, its sentences conflicting, some pushing others away, and its end contradicting its beginning. At one time it says: "Prescribed for you is fasting," at another it says: "It is permissible for the capable among you to break the fast and pay a ransom," and at another it says: "Fasting is obligatory upon all of you if you witness the month," thus abrogating the ruling of ransom for the capable while the ruling for the incapable remains as it was—yet there was no ruling for the incapable in the verse! Unless it is said: "they find it hard" (يطيقون) indicated capability before the abrogation, then it came to indicate the lack of capability after the abrogation. In short, according to this, His saying: "and for those who find it hard" in the middle of the verses must be an abrogator for His saying: "Prescribed for you is fasting" at their beginning due to the contradiction, and the question remains as to why it was restricted to "finding it hard" without an apparent reason. Then His saying: "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it" at the end of the verses would be an abrogator for His saying: "and for those who find it hard" in the middle, and the question remains as to why it abrogated the ruling for those capable of fasting only, excluding the incapacitated, while the abrogator is absolute and inclusive of both the capable and the incapacitated, and the abrogated does not include the ruling of the incapacitated whose continuation is intended. This is among the most heinous of errors.

If you add to this abrogation after abrogation what they mentioned regarding the abrogation of His saying: "The month of Ramaḍān..." for His saying: "for a fixed number of days...", and the abrogation of His saying: "for a fixed number of days..." for His saying: "Prescribed for you is fasting...", and you reflect on the meaning of the verses, you will witness something astonishing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فمن تطوع خيراً فهو خير له** (And whoever does good of his own accord, it is better for him): "Doing of one's own accord" (التطوع) is the *tafaʿʿul* form from "obedience" (الطوع), the opposite of "compulsion" (الكره). It is performing the action with pleasure and desire. The meaning of the *tafaʿʿul* pattern is adoption and acceptance. Thus, the meaning of *al-taṭawwuʿ* is to engage in performing the action with pleasure and desire without dislike or feeling burdened, whether it is a mandatory action or a non-mandatory one. As for the restriction of *al-taṭawwuʿ* in usage to recommended and supererogatory acts (المستحبات والمندوبات), this is something that occurred among Muslims after the revelation of the Quran, with the notion that the action performed with pleasure is the recommendation, while the obligatory has a tinge of dislike due to the compulsion within it.

In short, *al-taṭawwuʿ*, as has been said, has no indication in its root or form toward recommendation. Accordingly, the "fa" (ف) is for derivation, and the sentence is derived from the sum of the previous meaning of the speech. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: Fasting is prescribed for you, observing your good and welfare, along with your stability in the ranks of the nations before you, and mitigation and facilitation for you. So perform it willingly, not reluctantly, for whoever performs good willingly, it is better for him than performing it reluctantly.

From here it becomes apparent that His saying: "And whoever does good of his own accord" is of the type of placing the cause in the place of the effect—that is, placing the fact that *al-taṭawwuʿ* in absolute good is good in the place of the fact that *al-taṭawwuʿ* in fasting is good. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **قَدْ نَعْلَمُ إِنَّهُ لَيَحْزُنُكَ الَّذِي يَقُولُونَ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَا يُكَذِّبُونَكَ وَلَٰكِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ يَجْحَدُونَ** "We certainly know that what they say grieves you. Yet they do not call you a liar, but the wrongdoers deny the signs of Allah" (al-An'am: 33), meaning: so be patient and do not grieve, for they do not call you a liar.

It is sometimes said that the sentence—meaning His saying, the Exalted: "And whoever does good of his own accord, it is better for him"—is connected to the sentence that follows it, meaning His saying: "and for those who find it hard, a redemption by feeding a needy person." The meaning would be that whoever does good of his own accord regarding the ransom of feeding a needy person by giving more than the food for one needy person, such as the equivalent of two ransoms for two needy persons or for one, it is better for him.

This is refuted by: the lack of evidence for restricting *al-taṭawwuʿ* to recommendations as you have known, along with the obscurity of the point in the derivation. For no rational reason appears for the derivation of *al-taṭawwuʿ* by increase upon the ruling of the ransom. Moreover, His saying: "And whoever does good of his own accord" has no indication of *al-taṭawwuʿ* by increase, for doing good of one's own accord is different from doing an increase of one's own accord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وأن تصوموا خير لكم إن كنتم تعلمون** (and that you fast is better for you, should you know): A sentence completing its predecessor. The meaning according to the implied sense—as mentioned—is: perform the fasting prescribed for you willingly, for doing good willingly is good, and fasting is good for you, so doing it willingly is good upon good.

It is sometimes said that the sentence—meaning His saying: "and that you fast is better for you"—is an address to those with excuses, rather than the general body of believers addressed by the obligation and prescription. This is because its apparent meaning is the preference (رجحان) of the act of fasting, which does not prevent its omission, thus suiting recommendation (الاستحباب) rather than obligation. It is then interpreted as the preference and recommendation of fasting for the possessors of concession among the sick and the travelers; thus, it is recommended for them to choose fasting over breaking the fast and making it up.

This is refuted by: the lack of evidence for it, **firstly**. The difference between the two sentences—meaning His saying: "So should any of you..." and His saying: "and that you fast is better for you"—in terms of third-person and second-person address, **secondly**. That the first sentence is aimed at explaining concession and choice—rather, the apparent meaning of His saying: "let it be a [fixed] number of other days" is the specific determination of fasting in other days, as mentioned, **thirdly**. That the first sentence, assuming it came to explain concession for the excused, did not mention fasting and breaking the fast such that His saying: "and that you fast is better for you" would be an exposition for one of the two sides of the choice; rather, it only mentioned the fast of the month of Ramaḍān and the fast of a number of other days. In that case, there is no way to benefit the preference of the Ramaḍān fast over the fast of others from the mere saying: "and that you fast is better for you" without an apparent clue, **fourthly**. And that the context is not a context of explaining the ruling such that the appearance of preference would contradict the ruling being obligatory; rather, the context—as previously mentioned—is the context of the criterion of legislation (ملاك التشريع) and that the legislated ruling is not devoid of interest, good, and beauty, as in His saying: **فَتُوبُوا إِلَىٰ بَارِئِكُمْ فَاقْتُلُوا أَنفُسَكُمْ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ** "so turn penitenly to your Maker, and slay your own souls. That will be better for you" (al-Baqarah: 54), and His saying, the Exalted: **فَاسْعَوْا إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ وَذَرُوا الْبَيْعَ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** "hasten to the remembrance of Allah and leave all business. That is better for you, should you know" (al-Jumu'ah: 9), and His saying, the Exalted: **تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتُجَاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِأَمْوَالِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** "You have faith in Allah and His Apostle, and wage jihad in the way of Allah with your possessions and your persons. That is better for you, should you know" (al-Saff: 11). The verses in this regard are many, **fifthly**.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **شهر رمضان الذي أُنزل فيه القرآن هدى** (The month of Ramaḍān is every one in which the Quran was sent down as guidance): The month of Ramaḍān is the ninth month of the Arabic lunar months, between Shaʿbān and Shawwāl. No name of any of the months is mentioned in the Quran except the month of Ramaḍān.

"Descent" (النزول) is the arrival at a place from above. The difference between *al-inzāl* (الإِنزال) and *al-tanzīl* (التنزيل) is that *al-inzāl* is instantaneous (دفعي) while *al-tanzīl* is gradual (تدريجي). "The Quran" (القرآن) is the name for the Book revealed to His Prophet Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in consideration of its being recited, as He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** "We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason" (al-Zukhruf: 3). It is applied to the whole of the Book and to its parts.

The verse indicates the descent of the Quran in the month of Ramaḍān. Yet He, the Exalted, has said: **وَقُرْآنًا فَرَقْنَاهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْنَاهُ تَنزِيلًا** "As a Quran We have unfolded it [gradually], that you may recite it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in stages" (al-Isra: 106). This is apparent in its gradual descent over the entire period of the mission, which is approximately twenty-three years. Mutawātir history indicates this. Therefore, an objection is sometimes raised regarding the contradiction between the two verses.

It is sometimes answered: that it descended all at once (دفعة) to the lowest heaven in the month of Ramaḍān, then it descended upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in installments and at intervals over the period of twenty-three years—the total duration of the mission. This is an answer taken from narrations, some of which we will mention in the discussion of narrations.

It has been objected to this: that following His saying, the Exalted: "in which the Quran was sent down" with His saying: "as guidance for mankind and [as] manifest proofs of guidance and the Criterion" does not support this, as there is no meaning for its remaining with the description of guidance and the Criterion in the heaven for years.

It was answered: that its being a guide means it is its nature to guide those who need its guidance from misguidance, and its being a criterion when truth is confused with falsehood does not contradict its remaining for a period in the state of potentiality (الشأنية) without actualization of effect (فعلية التأثير) until its appointed time arrives. There are parallels and examples for this in organized civil laws, which, whenever the time for one of their articles arrives, are implemented and move from potentiality to actuality.

The truth is that the ruling of laws and constitutions is different from the ruling of addresses (الخطابات), which cannot possibly precede the station of addressing even for a short time. In the Quran, there are many verses of this kind, such as His saying, the Exalted: **قَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجَادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِهَا وَتَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحَاوُرَكُمَا** "Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah, and Allah hears your conversation" (al-Mujadilah: 1), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذَا رَأَوْا تِجَارَةً أَوْ لَهْوًا انفَضُّوا إِلَيْهَا وَتَرَكُوكَ قَائِمًا** "When they sight some merchandise or amusement, they scatter toward it and leave you standing" (al-Jumu'ah: 11), and His saying, the Exalted: **رِجَالٌ صَدَقُوا مَا عَاهَدُوا اللَّهَ عَلَيْهِ فَمِنْهُم مَّن قَضَىٰ نَحْبَهُ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَنتَظِرُ وَمَا بَدَّلُوا تَبْدِيلًا** "Among the faithful are men who fulfill what they have pledged to Allah: there are some among them who have fulfilled their pledge, and some of them who still wait, and they have not changed in the least" (al-Ahzab: 23). Furthermore, in the Quran there is the abrogating (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ), and there is no meaning for their meeting at one time in terms of descent.

It is sometimes answered regarding the objection: that what is meant by the descent of the Quran in the month of Ramaḍān is that the first of it to descend, descended in it. This is refuted by: the well-known view among them that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was only commissioned with the Quran, and he was commissioned on the twenty-seventh day of the month of Rajab, and between it and Ramaḍān is more than thirty days. How could the mission in this period be devoid of the descent of the Quran? Moreover, the beginning of Surah *Iqra' bi-ismi rabbika* bears witness that it was the first surah to descend and that it descended accompanying the mission. Likewise, Surah *al-Muddaththir* bears witness that it descended at the beginning of the call. In any case, it is very unlikely that the first verse descended in the month of Ramaḍān. Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted: "in which the Quran was sent down" is not explicit in indicating that what is meant by the Quran is the first part of it to descend, nor is there a clue in the speech indicating it; thus, carrying it upon that is an interpretation without evidence. Similar to this verse is His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُّبَارَكَةٍ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنذِرِينَ** "By the Manifest Book! We sent it down on a blessed night, and We have been warning [mankind]" (al-Dukhan: 2-3), and His saying: **إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ** "Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment" (al-Qadr: 1). The apparent meaning of these verses does not suit the meaning of the descent of the Quran being its first descent or the descent of the first of its parts, nor is there a clue in the speech indicating that.

What reflection upon the verses of the Book yields is another matter. For the verses speaking of the descent of the Quran in the month of Ramaḍān or in a night of it have expressed that with the word *al-inzāl* (الإِنزال), which indicates instantaneousness (الدفعة), rather than *al-tanzīl* (التنزيل), such as His saying, the Exalted: **شَهْرُ رَمَضَانَ الَّذِي أُنزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْآنُ** "The month of Ramaḍān is every one in which the Quran was sent down" (al-Baqarah: 185), and His saying, the Exalted: **حم وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُّبَارَكَةٍ** "Ha Mim. By the Manifest Book! We sent it down on a blessed night" (al-Dukhan: 1-3), and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ** "Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment" (al-Qadr: 1). The consideration of instantaneousness is either in view of considering the whole in the Book or the part descending from it, as in His saying, the Exalted: **كَمَاءٍ أَنزَلْنَاهُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ** "like the water We send down from the sky" (Yunus: 24). For rain only descends gradually, but the view here is directed toward taking it as a single whole; therefore, it was expressed with *al-inzāl* rather than *al-tanzīl*. And as in His saying, the Exalted: **كِتَابٌ أَنزَلْنَاهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَارَكٌ لِّيَدَّبَّرُوا آيَاتِهِ** "[This is] a blessed Book that We have sent down to you, so that they may contemplate its signs" (Sad: 29). Or it is because the Book possesses another reality (حقيقة) beyond what we understand with ordinary understanding—in which separation, detail, expansion, and gradualness are judged—which is what makes it correct for it to be one, non-gradual, and descending by *al-inzāl* rather than *al-tanzīl*. This second possibility is what shines from the noble verses, such as His saying, the Exalted: **كِتَابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آيَاتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ** "[This is] a Book whose signs have been made definitive and then elaborated from the presence of One who is All-wise, All-aware" (Hud: 1). For this "making definitive" (الإحكام) is the opposite of "elaboration" (التفصيل). Elaboration is making it part by part and piece by piece. Thus, "making definitive" is its being such that no part is separated from another part, nor is one distinguished from another, because of its return to a single meaning in which there are no parts or sections. The verse is explicit that this elaboration observed in the Quran only occurred to it after its being definitive and non-elaborated.

Clearer than it is His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ جِئْنَاهُم بِكِتَابٍ فَصَّلْنَاهُ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا تَأْوِيلَهُ يَوْمَ يَأْتِي تَأْوِيلُهُ يَقُولُ الَّذِينَ نَسُوهُ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُ رَبِّنَا بِالْحَقِّ** "Certainly We have brought them a Book, which We have elaborated with knowledge, as a guidance and mercy for a people who have faith. Do they await anything but its interpretation? The day its interpretation comes, those who had forgotten it before will say, ‘Our Lord’s apostles had certainly brought the truth’" (al-A'raf: 52-53). And His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا كَانَ هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنُ أَن يُفْتَرَىٰ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ وَلَٰكِن تَصْدِيقَ الَّذِي بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَتَفْصِيلَ الْكِتَابِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ مِن رَّبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "This Quran could not have been fabricated by anyone besides Allah; rather, it is a confirmation of what was [revealed] before it, and an elaboration of the Book, there is no doubt in it, from the Lord of all the worlds" — until He said: — **بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِمَا لَمْ يُحِيطُوا بِعِلْمِهِ وَلَمَّا يَأْتِهِمْ تَأْوِيلُهُ** "Rather, they deny that whose knowledge they do not comprehend, and whose interpretation has not yet come to them" (Yunus: 37-39). For the noble verses, especially what is in Surah Yunus, are apparent in indicating that elaboration (التفصيل) is an occurrence upon the Book; thus, the Book itself is one thing and the elaboration that occurs to it is another thing. And they only denied the elaboration of the Book because they were forgetful of something to which this elaboration returns and were heedless of it. It will become apparent to them on the Day of Resurrection and they will be forced to know it, but regret will not benefit them, and it will not be the time for escape. In this, there is an intimation that the origin of the Book is the interpretation (تأويل) of the elaboration of the Book.

Clearer still is His saying, the Exalted: **حم وَالْكِتَابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتَابِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** "Ha Mim. By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother of the Book, sublime and wise" (al-Zukhruf: 1-4). For it is apparent in that there is a Manifest Book (كتاب مبين) upon which was occurred its being made a recited Arabic [Quran]. It was only clothed in the garment of recitation and Arabic so that people might understand it; otherwise, it—while in the Mother of the Book (أُمّ الكتاب)—is with Allah, sublime (عليّ), such that intellects do not ascend to it, and wise (حكيم), such that no separation or section is found in it. In the verse, there is a definition of the Manifest Book and that it is the origin of the Manifest Arabic Quran. In this same course is also His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِمَوَاقِعِ النُّجُومِ وَإِنَّهُ لَقَسَمٌ لَّوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ لَّا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "I swear by the places where the stars set! And indeed it is a great oath, should you know. This is indeed a noble Quran, in a guarded Book—no one touches it except the pure—a gradual sending down from the Lord of all the worlds" (al-Waqi'ah: 75-80). For it is apparent in that for the Quran there is a location, which is in the Guarded Book (كتاب مكنون), where no one touches it except the purified from among the servants of Allah, and that the gradual sending down (التنزيل) is after it. As for before the gradual sending down, it has a location in a Book guarded from strangers, which is what was expressed in the verses of al-Zukhruf as the "Mother of the Book" (أم الكتاب), and in Surah al-Buruj as the "Preserved Tablet" (اللوح المحفوظ), where He, the Exalted, said: **بَلْ هُوَ قُرْآنٌ مَّجِيدٌ فِي لَوْحٍ مَّحْفُوظٍ** "Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet" (al-Buruj: 21-22). This Tablet was only "preserved" (محفوظاً) to protect it from the arrival of change upon it. It is well known that the Quran sent down gradually is not devoid of the abrogating and the abrogated, and of gradualness which is a type of change. Thus, the Manifest Book, which is the origin of the Quran and its definitive ruling (حكمه) devoid of elaboration, is something beyond this sent-down [Quran], and this is merely like the garment for that.

Furthermore, this meaning—namely, that the Quran in the stage of gradual sending down (التنزيل) in relation to the Manifest Book (which we call the reality of the Book) is like the garment to the wearer, like the image to the reality, and like the parable to the purpose intended by the speech—is what makes it correct for "the Quran" to be applied sometimes to the origin of the Book, as in His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet," and so on. This which we have mentioned is what necessitates carrying His saying: "The month of Ramaḍān in which the Quran was sent down," and His saying: "Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night," and His saying: "Indeed We sent it down on the Night of Ordainment," upon the descent of the reality of the Book and the Manifest Book to the heart of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) all at once, just as the elaborated Quran was sent down to his heart gradually over the period of the prophetic mission.

This is what shines from the likes of His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَعْجَلْ بِالْقُرْآنِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يُقْضَىٰ إِلَيْكَ وَحْيُهُ** "and do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed for you" (Ta Ha: 114), and His saying, the Exalted: **لَا تُحرِّكْ بِهِ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُ وَقُرْآنَهُ فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَاهُ فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُ** "Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it. Indeed it is up to Us to put it together and to recite it. And when We have recited it, follow its recitation. Then its exposition is also up to Us" (al-Qiyamah: 16-19). For the verses are apparent in that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had knowledge of what would be sent down to him, so he was forbidden from hastening with the recitation before the completion of the revelation. Its clarification will come in its appropriate place—if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

In short, the one who reflects upon the Quranic verses finds no escape from admitting their indication: that this Quran sent down to the Prophet gradually is based upon a reality (حقيقة) too exalted for the eyes of common intellects to perceive or the hands of thoughts polluted with the stains of whims and the filth of matter to reach. And that reality was sent down (أُنزلت) to the Prophet, so Allah taught him thereby the reality of what He meant by His Book. Some of the speech related to this meaning will come in the discussion of interpretation (التأويل) and gradual sending down (التنزيل) in His saying, the Exalted: **هُوَ الَّذِي أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ مِنْهُ آيَاتٌ مُّحْكَمَاتٌ** "It is He who has sent down to you the Book. Parts of it are definitive signs" (Al ʿImran: 7). This is what reflection guides to and the verses indicate. Yes, the people of hadith, the majority of the theologians, and the empiricists among the researchers of this era, when they denied the reality of what lies beyond sensible matter, were forced to carry these verses and their likes—such as those indicating the Quran being guidance, mercy, light, spirit, the places of the stars, a Manifest Book, in a Preserved Tablet, descending from Allah, and in purified scrolls, among other realities—upon types of metaphor and figurative speech. Thus, the Quran returned, in their view, to being prose poetry.

One of the researchers has a discourse on the meaning of the descent of the Quran in the month of Ramaḍān:

He said, in summary: There is no doubt that the mission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was concurrent with the descent of the first of what descended of the Quran and his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being commanded to convey and warn. There is no doubt that this event occurred at night, due to His saying, the Exalted: "Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night, and We have been warning [mankind]" (al-Dukhan: 3). And there is no doubt that the night was among the nights of the month of Ramaḍān, due to His saying, the Exalted: "The month of Ramaḍān is every one in which the Quran was sent down" (al-Baqarah: 185).

Although the entirety of the Quran did not descend in that night, since Surah *al-Ḥamd* descended in it—and it contains the summaries of the Quran's knowledge—it was as if the Quran descended in it entirely. Thus, it was correct to say: "We sent it down in a night" (on the basis that "the Quran" is applied to the part as it is applied to the whole; rather, "the Quran" is applied to the other heavenly books as well, such as the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms, in the terminology of the Quran).

He said: That is because the first of what descended of the Quran was His saying, the Exalted: "Read in the name of your Lord..." etc. It descended on the night of the twenty-fifth of the month of Ramaḍān. It descended while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was heading to the house of Khadījah in the middle of the valley, witnessing Gabriel, who revealed to him: His saying, the Exalted: "Read in the name of your Lord who created..." etc. When he received the revelation, it occurred to his mind to ask him: how should he mention the name of his Lord? So he appeared to him and taught him by His saying: "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds..." to the end of Surah *al-Ḥamd*. Then he taught him the manner of prayer (الصلوة), then he vanished from his sight. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) then came to himself and found no trace of what he was witnessing except the fatigue that had overcome him from the pressure of Gabriel during the revelation. He proceeded on his way, not knowing that he was a messenger from Allah to the people, commanded to guide them. Then when he entered the house, he slept that night from the intensity of fatigue. The angel of revelation returned to him on the morning of that night and revealed to him His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ قُمْ فَأَنذِرْ** "O you wrapped up in your mantle! Arise and warn" (al-Muddaththir: 1-2).

He said: This is the meaning of the descent of the Quran in the month of Ramaḍān and the coincidence of his mission with the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر). As for what is found in some books of the Shiʿah that the mission was on the twenty-seventh day of the month of Rajab, these reports—besides being found only in some Shiʿah books whose date of composition does not precede the beginning of the fourth century of the Hijrah—are in opposition to the Book, as you have known.

He said: There are other narrations in support of these reports indicating that the meaning of the descent of the Quran in the month of Ramaḍān is that it descended in it before the mission of the Prophet from the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) to the Inhabited House (البيت المعمور), and Gabriel dictated it there to the angels so that it would descend after the mission upon the Messenger of Allah. These are superstitious delusions inserted into the reports, rejected firstly by opposition to the Book, and secondly because the Quran's meaning of the Preserved Tablet is the world of nature, and of the Inhabited House is the globe of the earth because of its being inhabited by the residence of man in it. (End of summary).

I do not know which sentence among the sentences of his discourse—despite its corruption in all its parts—accepts correction so as to correspond to the truth and reality in any way? The breach has become too wide for the mender.

In it, **firstly:** this strange fabrication he fabricated regarding the mission and the descent of the Quran—that "Read in the name of your Lord" descended upon him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) while he was on the road, then Surah *al-Ḥamd* descended, then prayer was taught, then he entered the house and slept tired, then Surah *al-Muddaththir* descended on the morning of the night and he was commanded to convey—all of that is fabrication for which there is no evidence, neither a definitive verse nor an established Sunnah. It is merely an imaginary story that does not agree with the Book nor with the transmission, as will follow.

**Secondly:** He mentioned that it is taken for granted that the mission, the descent of the Quran, and the command to convey were concurrent in time. Then he interpreted this by saying that the prophethood (النبوة) began with the descent of the Quran, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was a prophet but not a messenger for one night only, then on the morning of the night he was given the messengership (الرسالة) by the descent of Surah *al-Muddaththir*. He cannot rely in this upon Book or Sunnah, and it is not taken for granted. As for the Sunnah, because the necessity of what he used to disparage the Shiʿah collections—due to their composition being later than the occurrence of the event—is the lack of reliance upon any of the collections of hadith absolutely, since none of the books of hadith composed by the Sunnis or the Shiʿah were composed except that their composition was later than the era of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by two centuries or more. This is regarding the Sunnah. History—besides being devoid of these details—is in a worse state, and the insertion he accused the hadith of is also applicable to it.

As for the Book, the deficiency of its indication of what he mentioned is clearer and more manifest; rather, its indication of the opposite of what he mentioned and its lie against what he fabricated is apparent. For Surah *Iqra' bi-ismi rabbika*—which was the first surah to descend upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) according to what the people of transmission mentioned, and the five verses at its beginning bear witness to it, and no one mentioned that it descended in pieces, or at least the possibility of its descending all at once—contains that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to pray in the sight of the people, and that there were those among them who forbade him from prayer and mentioned his matter in the assembly of the people. (We do not know how this prayer was, by which the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to draw close to his Lord in his initial state, except for what this surah contains of the command for prostration). He, the Exalted, said in it: **أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يَنْهَىٰ عَبْدًا إِذَا صَلَّىٰ أَرَأَيْتَ إِن كَانَ عَلَى الْهُدَىٰ أَوْ أَمَرَ بِالتَّقْوَىٰ أَرَأَيْتَ إِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ أَلَمْ يَعْلَم بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ يَرَىٰ كَلَّا لَئِن لَّمْ يَنتَهِ لَنَسْفَعًا بِالنَّاصِيَةِ نَاصِيَةٍ كَاذِبَةٍ خَاطِئَةٍ فَلْيَدْعُ نَادِيَهُ سَنَدْعُ الزَّبَانِيَةَ** "Tell me, he who forbids a servant when he prays—tell me, if he is on [the path of] guidance, or bids godwariness—tell me, if he denies and turns away, does he not know that Allah sees? No indeed! If he does not stop, We shall seize him by the forelock, a lying, sinful forelock! Then let him call his council; We shall call the keepers of hell" (al-ʿAlaq: 9-18). The verses, as you see, are apparent in that there was someone there who forbade a prayer-performer from prayer, and mentioned his matter in the assembly, and did not stop his actions. This prayer-performer was the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by the evidence of His saying, the Exalted, after that: **كَلَّا لَا تُطِعْهُ** "No indeed! Do not obey him" (al-ʿAlaq: 19).

The Surah indicates that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) used to pray before the revelation of the first Surah of the Qur’an; he was already upon guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — and would occasionally enjoin godwariness (التقوى) — *the innate disposition of being conscious of Allah’s presence*. This state is precisely what constitutes prophethood (النبوة), even though his activity at that time was not yet termed "warning" (إنذاراً). Thus, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was a Prophet and used to pray while no part of the Qur’an had yet been revealed to him, nor had *Surat al-Hamd* been sent down, and he had not yet been commanded to convey the message (التبليغ).

As for *Surat al-Hamd*, it was revealed a period of time after that. Had its revelation followed *Surat al-ʿAlaq* immediately without any interval—as a mere thought occurring in the heart of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), as this researcher claims—the proper phrasing of the speech would have been: "Say: In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds..." or "In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. Say: All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds..." Furthermore, it would have been necessary to conclude the speech at His saying, the Exalted: **مالك يوم الدين** (Master of the Day of Retribution), because the remaining verses would fall outside the intended purpose, as is more fitting for the eloquence of the Noble Qur’an.

Indeed, it occurs in *Surat al-Hijr*—which is a Meccan Surah as indicated by the content of its verses, the explanation of which will follow—His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَاكَ سَبْعًا مِّنَ الْمَثَانِي وَالْقُرْآنَ الْعَظِيمَ** — "Certainly We have given you the seven of the oft-repeated [verses] and the great Quran" (15:87). What is meant by the "seven oft-repeated" (السبْع المثاني) is *Surat al-Hamd*. It has been placed in juxtaposition with the Great Qur’an, which constitutes the utmost glorification of its status and the magnification of its importance. However, it was not counted as the [entire] Qur’an, but rather as seven of the verses of the Qur’an and a part of it, as evidenced by His saying, the Exalted: **كِتَابًا مُّتَشَابِهًا مَّثَانِيَ** — "a Scripture consistent in its parts, and oft-repeated" (39:23).

Nevertheless, the inclusion of the mention of *Surat al-Hamd* in this Surah indicates that its revelation preceded the revelation of *Surat al-Hijr*. This Surah also contains His saying, the Exalted: **فَاصْدَعْ بِمَا تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ إِنَّا كَفَيْنَاكَ الْمُسْتَهْزِئِينَ** — "So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists. Indeed We will suffice you against the mockers" (15:94-95). This indicates that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) had refrained from warning for a period, then was commanded to do so a second time by His saying, the Exalted: "So proclaim" (فاصدع).

As for *Surat al-Muddaththir* and what it contains of His saying: **قُمْ فَأَنذِرْ** — "Arise and warn" (74:2), if the Surah was revealed in its entirety at once, then the status of this verse, "Arise and warn," is the same as His saying, the Exalted: "So proclaim what you have been commanded," because this Surah also contains His saying, the Exalted: **ذَرْنِي وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيدًا** — "Leave Me to deal with him whom I created alone" (74:11) until the end of the verses, which is close in meaning to His saying in *Surat al-Hijr*: "and turn away from the polytheists." If, however, the Surah was revealed in installments (نجوماً), then the apparent context (السياق) suggests that its beginning was revealed at the start of the Messengership (الرسالة).

**Thirdly:** His claim that the narrations indicating the revelation of the Qur’an on the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر) from the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) to the Inhabited House (البيت المعمور) as a single whole before the commencement of the mission, followed by the revelation of the verses in installments to the Messenger of Allah, are fabricated and mythical reports because they contradict the Book and their content is inconsistent—and that the "Preserved Tablet" refers to the world of nature (عالم الطبيعة) and the "Inhabited House" refers to the globe of the Earth—is an error and a fabrication.

**Firstly:** Because nothing in the apparent meaning of the Book contradicts these reports, as you have come to know.

**Secondly:** Because the reports are devoid of any mention that the collective revelation occurred before the commencement of the mission; rather, this phrase is something he added to their content without verification.

**Thirdly:** Because his saying that the Preserved Tablet is the world of nature is a hideous interpretation—indeed, it is a laughingstock. I wonder: what is the justifying reason—according to his view—for naming the world of nature in His speech, the Exalted, a "Preserved Tablet"? Is it because this world is preserved from change and transformation? Yet it is the world of movements, fluid in its essence (سيال الذات), and changing in its attributes! Or is it because it is preserved from corruption ontologically (تكوينًا) or legislatively (تشريعًا)? The reality is contrary to that! Or is it because it is preserved from the access of those other than its people? As indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَمٌ فِي كِتَابٍ مَّكْنُونٍ لَّا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ** — "This is indeed a noble Quran, in a guarded Book—no one touches it except the pure" (56:77-79). Yet the perception of all perceivers regarding it is equal!

After all is said and done, this researcher has not provided any coherent explanation for the revelation of the Qur’an in the month of Ramadan that the wording of the verse could accept. The sum of his explanation is that the meaning of "The Qur’an was revealed in it" is "as if the Qur’an was revealed in it," and the meaning of "Indeed, We revealed it in a night" is "as if We revealed it in a night." This is something that neither the language nor conventional usage (العرف) can tolerate in this context!

If it were permissible for a speaker to say: "The Qur’an was revealed on the Night of Ordainment to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) because of the revelation of *Surat al-Fatihah* which contains the sum of the Qur’anic gnosis," then it would be permissible to say: the meaning of the revelation of the Qur’an is its revelation as a single whole—that is, the revelation of the totality of its gnosis upon the heart of the Messenger of Allah without any hindrance, as previously explained.

There are other aspects of corruption in his discourse which we have refrained from discussing as they fall outside our present purpose.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ وَبَيِّنَاتٍ مِّنَ الْهُدَىٰ وَالْفُرْقَانِ** — "as a guidance for mankind and [as] clear proofs of guidance and the Criterion" (2:185). "Mankind" (الناس) refers to the lower stratum of humanity whose average level of understanding is the most superficial. This word is frequently used in their regard, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "but most of the people do not know" (30:30), and He, the Exalted, said: **وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ وَمَا يَعْقِلُهَا إِلَّا الْعَالِمُونَ** — "We draw these parables for mankind; but no one grasps them except those who have knowledge" (29:43). These are the people of imitation (أهل التقليد) who lack the capacity to distinguish spiritual matters through clear proof (البينة) and demonstration (البرهان), nor can they distinguish truth from falsehood through evidence except by an explicator who clarifies for them and a guide who leads them. The Qur’an is a guidance (هدى) for them, and what an excellent guidance it is. As for the elite (الخاصة) who have perfected themselves in both knowledge and action, and are ready to draw from the lights of divine guidance and rely upon the criterion of truth, the Qur’an constitutes "clear proofs" (بينات) and witnesses of guidance and the Criterion in their regard. It guides them to Him, distinguishes the truth for them, and explains to them how to distinguish. He, the Exalted, said: **يَهْدِي بِهِ اللَّهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوَانَهُ سُبُلَ السَّلَامِ وَيُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ الظُّلُمَاتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ** — "With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace, and brings them out from darkness into light by His will, and guides them to a straight path" (5:16).

From here, the reason for the juxtaposition between "guidance" and "clear proofs of guidance" becomes apparent; it is the juxtaposition between the general and the specific. Thus, "guidance" is for some, and "clear proofs of guidance" are for others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَن شَهِدَ مِنكُمُ الشَّهْرَ فَلْيَصُمْهُ** — "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it" (2:185). "Witnessing" (الشهادة) is presence (الحضور) accompanied by the acquisition of knowledge from its direction. Witnessing the month is simply by its arrival and the knowledge of it, and it can occur through a part of it just as it occurs through the whole. As for the claim that "witnessing the month" means seeing its crescent, or that the person must be a resident (بالحضر) as opposed to being on a journey, there is no evidence for this except by way of necessary implication (الملازمة) in certain times according to contextual clues, and there is no such clue in the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَن كَانَ مَرِيضًا أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ فَعِدَّةٌ مِّنْ أَيَّامٍ أُخَرَ** — "and as for him who is sick or on a journey, let it be a [similar] number of other days" (2:185). The inclusion of this sentence in the verse a second time is not a case of repetition for emphasis or the like. This is because, as you have seen, the two previous verses, along with what they contain, were intended as a preamble and preparation (توطئة وتمهيد) rather than a final statement of the ruling. The ruling is what has been clarified in the third verse; therefore, there is no repetition.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ بِكُمُ الْيُسْرَ وَلَا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ الْعُسْرَ وَلِتُكْمِلُوا الْعِدَّةَ** — "Allah desires ease for you, and He does not desire hardship for you, and so that you may complete the number" (2:185). This appears to be an explanation for the sum of the ruling of exception: which is breaking the fast in the month of Ramadan due to the negation of hardship, and fasting a number of other days due to the obligation of completing the count. The "Lām" in His saying "so that you may complete the number" (لتكملوا العدة) is for the purpose (للغاية), and it is coordinated with His saying "He desires" (يريد) because it contains the meaning of purpose. The implied meaning is: "We have only commanded you to break the fast and make it up to lighten your burden and so that you may complete the count." Perhaps the inclusion of His saying "and so that you may complete the number" is what necessitated the omission of the meaning of His saying "and for those who find it hard, a redemption by feeding a needy person" from this verse, while its ruling is understood through the negation of hardship and its mention in the previous verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِتُكَبِّرُوا اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَاكُمْ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** — "and so that you may magnify Allah for guiding you, and that you may give thanks" (2:185). The apparent meaning of the two phrases, as suggested by the "Lām" of purpose, is that they state the purpose—the purpose of the fast itself rather than the ruling of exception. For the qualification of His saying "the month of Ramadan" with His saying "in which the Qur’an was revealed" until the end, suggests a kind of causality (علية) and a connection between the obligation of fasting the month of Ramadan and the revelation of the Qur’an as a guidance for mankind and clear proofs of guidance and the Criterion. Thus, the meaning of the purpose returns to the fact that engaging in the fast is to manifest His greatness, the Exalted, for what He revealed to them of the Qur’an and His proclamation of His Lordship and their servitude, and as thanks to Him for guiding them to the truth and distinguishing for them through His Book between truth and falsehood. Since fasting is only characterized as being "thanks" for His blessings when it contains the reality of the meaning of fasting—which is sincerity (الإخلاص) to Allah, the Glorified, in transcending the pollutions of nature and refraining from the greatest desires of the soul—unlike its characterization as "magnifying" (التكبير) Allah, for the outward form of fasting and refraining, whether it contains sincerity of intention or not, indicates His magnification and glorification, a distinction was made between magnification and thanks. Thus, thanks was coupled with the word of hope (الترجي) unlike magnification, so He said: "and so that you may magnify Allah for guiding you, and that you may give thanks," just as He said at the beginning of the verses: "so that you may be God-conscious" (لعلكم تتقون).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Hadith al-Qudsi*, Allah the Exalted said: **الصوم لي وأنا اجزي به** — "Fasting is for Me, and I [Myself] am its reward."

**I say:** This has been narrated by both groups [Sunni and Shi'a] with slight variations. The reason for fasting being for Allah, the Glorified, is that it is the only act of worship composed of negation (النفي). Others, such as prayer, pilgrimage, and so on, are composed of affirmation (الإثبات) or are not devoid of affirmation. An existential act (الفعل الوجودي) does not purely manifest the servitude of the servant nor the Lordship of the Lord, the Glorified, because it is not free from the taint of material deficiency, the affliction of limitation, and the assertion of the ego (إثبات الإنية). It is possible for someone other than Allah, the Exalted, to have a share in it, as in cases of ostentation (الرياء), seeking reputation (السمعة), and prostration to other than Him. This is in contrast to the negation contained in fasting—by rising above being anchored to the earth and purifying oneself by refraining from the desires of the soul. For negation, no one other than Allah, the Exalted, has a share in it, because it is a matter between the servant and the Lord that, by its nature, no one else can observe. His saying "I [Myself] am its reward" (أنا اجزي به), if read in the active voice (*ajzī*), indicates that He does not place anyone as a mediator between Himself and the fasting person in giving the reward, just as the servant performs that which no one else observes between him and his Lord. This is similar to what has been narrated: that Allah Himself takes the charity (الصدقة) without mediating anyone; He, the Exalted, said: **وَيَأْخُذُ الصَّدَقَاتِ** — "and receives the charities" (9:104). If it is read in the passive voice (*ujzā*), it is a metaphor for the fact that the reward of the fasting person is proximity to Allah, the Exalted.

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام): "When the Messenger of Allah was first sent, he would fast until it was said: 'He does not break his fast,' and he would break his fast until it was said: 'He does not fast.' Then he left that and fasted one day and broke his fast one day, which is the fast of David. Then he left that and fasted the three 'white days' (الأيام الغر). Then he left that and distributed them such that in every ten days there was one day: two Thursdays with a Wednesday between them. He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) passed away while practicing that."

And from ʿAnbasah the Worshipper, he said: "The Messenger of Allah passed away while fasting Shaʿban, Ramadan, and three days of every month."

**I say:** The reports from the channels of the Ahl al-Bayt are numerous regarding this, and it is the recommended fast (الصوم المسنون) that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) used to observe besides the fast of Ramadan.

In *Tafsir al-ʿAyyashi*, from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الصِّيَامُ** — "O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fasting" (2:183). He said: "It is for the believers specifically."

From Jamil, he said: "I asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted: 'O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fighting,' and 'O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fasting.' He said: 'All of these include the misguided, the hypocrites, and everyone who has acknowledged the outward call.'"

In *al-Faqih*, from Hafs, who said: "I heard Abu ʿAbdillah (عليه‌السلام) saying: 'Indeed, Allah did not prescribe the fasting of the month of Ramadan for any of the nations before us.' I said to him: 'Then what of the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "O you who have faith! Prescribed for you is fasting as it was prescribed for those before you"?' He said: 'Allah only prescribed the month of Ramadan for the Prophets and not the nations. Thus, Allah favored this nation and made its fasting an obligation upon the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and upon his nation.'"

**I say:** The narration is weak due to Ismaʿil ibn Muhammad in its chain. This meaning has also been narrated as a *mursal* report from "the Scholar" (Imam al-Kazim) (عليه‌السلام), and it is as if the two narrations are one. In any case, it is among the solitary reports (الآحاد), and the apparent meaning of the verse does not support the idea that "those before you" refers to the Prophets specifically. Had it been so, and given that the context is one of preamble, preparation, encouragement, and motivation, the explicit mention of their name would have been more appropriate and effective than a metaphor. And Allah knows best.

In *al-Kafi*, from one who asked Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) about the "Qur’an" and the "Criterion" (الفرقان): "Are they two things or one thing?" He said: "The Qur’an is the entirety of the Book, and the Criterion is the decisive ruling (الحكم الواجب) that must be acted upon."

In *al-Jawamiʿ*, from him (عليه‌السلام): "The Criterion is every clear verse (آية محكمة) in the Book."

In the *Tafsirs* of al-ʿAyyashi and al-Qummi, from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم): "The Criterion is every clear matter in the Qur’an, and the Book is the entirety of the Qur’an which confirms the Prophets who were before it."

**I say:** The wording supports this. In some reports, it is mentioned that "Ramadan" is one of the names of Allah, the Exalted, so it should not be said: "Ramadan has come" or "has gone," but rather "the month of Ramadan," etc. This is a unique and rare report in its category. This statement has also been transmitted from Qatadah among the commentators.

The reports detailing His names, the Exalted, are devoid of the mention of "Ramadan." Furthermore, the use of the word "Ramadan" without being preceded by the word "month," as well as "two Ramadans" in the dual form, occurs frequently in the narrations transmitted from the Prophet and from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام), such that it is very unlikely to attribute the omission to the narrator.

In *Tafsir al-ʿAyyashi*, from al-Sabbah ibn Nubatah, who said: "I said to Abu ʿAbdillah (عليه‌السلام): 'Ibn Abi Yaʿfur commanded me to ask you about some issues.' He said: 'And what are they?' I said: 'He says to you: If the month of Ramadan enters while I am in my home, is it for me to travel?' He said: 'Indeed, Allah says: "So let those of you who witness it fast [in] it." So whoever the month of Ramadan enters upon while he is with his family, he has no right to travel except for Hajj, or ʿUmrah, or in search of wealth he fears will be lost.'"

**I say:** This is a subtle derivation of a recommended ruling (حكم استحبابي) by taking the general application (الاطلاق).

In *al-Kafi*, from Imam ʿAli ibn al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام), who said: "As for fasting during travel and illness, the commonality (العامة) [non-Shi'a] have differed regarding that.
